content:
4 min read
1 hour ago

--

I’ve wasted money on bad templates. Not once — multiple times. Templates that looked stunning in demos but were held together with jQuery duct tape underneath. Templates labeled “Angular 17” that had jquery.min.js sitting right there in the vendor folder. Templates with 3000-line components and zero folder structure.
After building and selling templates since 2021, I’ve seen both sides — as a buyer and as a seller. Here’s what I actually check before purchasing any admin dashboard template now.
1. Open the package.json First — Not the Demo
I know. The demo looks incredible. The sidebar animation is smooth, the charts are beautiful, the dark mode is perfect. Don’t buy it yet.
Find the package.json. Ask the seller if it’s not publicly available. Because I’ve seen this more times than I’d like:
{
"dependencies": {
"@angular/core": "^17.0.0",
"jquery": "^3.6.0",
"bootstrap": "^4.6.0"
}
}Angular 17. jQuery. Bootstrap 4. In 2026. And the listing said “Bootstrap 5 Angular template.”
What you want to see:
{
"dependencies": {
"@angular/core": "^22.0.0",
"bootstrap": "^5.3.0"
}
}No jQuery. Current Angular. Current Bootstrap. If jQuery is in there — walk away regardless of how good the demo looks. You’ll spend weeks untangling it.
2. Test the Demo on Your Actual Phone
Not Chrome DevTools. Not the responsive toggle in Firefox. Your actual phone.
Every template claims to be fully responsive. Half of them aren’t — not properly. Here’s what breaks on mobile that DevTools won’t always catch:
Open the sidebar on mobile. Does it actually close when you tap outside it? Does it overlay content properly or does it push everything sideways and create horizontal scroll?
Open a data table. Can you scroll it horizontally without the entire page layout breaking? On some templates the table overflow breaks the body scroll entirely — fun to discover after you’ve integrated three months of real data into it.
Tap the navbar toggle. Does it work on first tap or does it require two taps because some JavaScript event is interfering?
Five minutes on your phone before purchasing saves days of CSS debugging after.
3. Run Lighthouse — Then Decide
This one surprises people. Why does template performance matter if you’re going to add your own code anyway?
Because a template that scores 45 on Lighthouse before you’ve added anything is going to score 30 after you’ve added your real data and features. You’re inheriting its performance problems.
Open the live demo. Chrome DevTools → Lighthouse → Analyze page load. Look at the Performance score.
Below 70 — be cautious. Ask why it’s slow before buying.
Below 50 — serious red flag. That’s a slow template before your code touches it.
Common culprits I’ve seen in templates with terrible Lighthouse scores:

• Six different icon libraries all loaded simultaneously

• 4MB of unoptimized hero images

• All JavaScript loaded upfront with zero lazy loading

• Three different jQuery plugins running on every page
One template I evaluated had a 31 Lighthouse score on its own demo page. The seller’s response when I asked was “it’s a demo, production will be different.” It won’t be.
4. Read the Code — Even Just 10 Minutes
I get it. You want to buy a template precisely because you don’t want to spend time on UI. But spending 10 minutes reading actual component code before buying saves you from discovering after purchase that you’re working with this:
// actual code from a "premium" Angular template I evaluated
export class AppComponent {
// 2847 lines
ngOnInit() {
$(document).ready(function() {
// jQuery inside an Angular component
});
}
}That’s a real example. One component. 2847 lines. jQuery inside ngOnInit. This was sold as a premium Angular template.
What good code looks like — components under 200 lines, clear folder structure, TypeScript interfaces defined properly, SCSS variables in one place. What bad code looks like — you’ll know it when you see it. Trust that instinct.
If the seller has a GitHub org or public repo — check it. If they offer a free version — download it and look at the source before buying premium.
5. Check the Last Update Date
A template last updated in 2022 is technical debt you’re buying. Angular 22, React 19, Next.js 15 — the frameworks have changed enormously in two years. An unmaintained template means:

• Dependency conflicts when you try to add new packages

• Security vulnerabilities in outdated dependencies

• Missing framework features you’ll want to use

• No support when something breaks
Look for a changelog. Look for recent commits if there’s a GitHub repo. Look for whether the seller responds to support questions.
I’ve seen templates where the last update was “fixed minor CSS issue” three years ago. That’s not maintenance — that’s abandonment with a listing still active.
Active maintenance is worth paying more for. A $30 template that gets quarterly updates beats a $12 template that hasn’t been touched since Angular 14.
The Actual Checklist I Use
Before I buy any template now:

• package.json shows Angular 22 / React 19 / Next.js 15

• Zero jQuery in dependencies

• Tested on real mobile — sidebar, tables, modals all work

• Lighthouse performance above 70

• Component code is readable — under 200 lines per component

• Updated within last 6 months

• Seller responds to support questions
One More Thing
Price is not quality. I’ve used $8 templates that were beautifully written. I’ve used $59 templates that were jQuery nightmares. The checklist above matters more than the price tag.
Spend five minutes on due diligence. It saves weeks of regret.
If you want Angular 22 and React 19 templates that pass every item on this list — TypeScript-first, zero jQuery, Signals, standalone components, updated regularly — take a look at LettStart Design. Use code FIRST30 for 30% off your first purchase.
